full_model_response:
The degenerate subspace at unperturbed energy \(2\hbar\omega\) is spanned by
\[
|1,0\rangle,\qquad |0,1\rangle,
\]
where \(E^0_{n_x,n_y}=\hbar\omega(n_x+n_y+1)\).

Write
\[
xy=\frac{\hbar}{2m\omega}(a_x^\dagger+a_x)(a_y^\dagger+a_y),
\]
so in this two-dimensional degenerate subspace,
\[
H'_{\rm deg}=\frac{K\hbar}{2m\omega}
\begin{pmatrix}
0&1\\
1&0
\end{pmatrix}.
\]
Thus the correct zeroth-order states are the symmetric and antisymmetric combinations. Acting with \(H'\) on those states only connects outside the degenerate subspace to \(|2,1\rangle\) and \(|1,2\rangle\), whose unperturbed energy is \(4\hbar\omega\).

\[
\boxed{
\begin{gathered}
\text{Let } \lambda=\frac{K\hbar}{2m\omega},\qquad
|+\rangle^{(0)}=\frac{|1,0\rangle+|0,1\rangle}{\sqrt2},\qquad
|-\rangle^{(0)}=\frac{|1,0\rangle-|0,1\rangle}{\sqrt2}.\\[3pt]
\text{(a) First-order energy corrections:}\qquad
E_+^{(1)}=+\lambda=\frac{K\hbar}{2m\omega},\qquad
E_-^{(1)}=-\lambda=-\frac{K\hbar}{2m\omega}.\\[3pt]
\text{(b) Second-order energy corrections:}\qquad
E_+^{(2)}=E_-^{(2)}
=-\frac{\lambda^2}{\hbar\omega}
=-\frac{K^2\hbar}{4m^2\omega^3}.\\[3pt]
\text{Equivalently, through second order:}\qquad
E_\pm=2\hbar\omega\pm\frac{K\hbar}{2m\omega}
-\frac{K^2\hbar}{4m^2\omega^3}+O(K^3).\\[3pt]
\text{(c) First-order wavefunction corrections, choosing no component inside the degenerate subspace:}\\[2pt]
|\psi_+^{(1)}\rangle
=-\frac{K}{4m\omega^2}\bigl(|2,1\rangle+|1,2\rangle\bigr),\qquad
|\psi_-^{(1)}\rangle
=-\frac{K}{4m\omega^2}\bigl(|2,1\rangle-|1,2\rangle\bigr).\\[3pt]
\text{Thus }|\psi_\pm\rangle=|\pm\rangle^{(0)}+|\psi_\pm^{(1)}\rangle+O(K^2).
\end{gathered}
} 
\]

review_note: Rank 76 among non-visual hardest rows by lowest best@4. Best attempt repeat 1 scored 0.3333333333333333; mean@4=0.333, best@4=0.333.
question:
(i) The decay $K \to \pi \gamma$ is absolutely forbidden by a certain conservation law, which is believed to hold exactly. Which conservation law is this?

(iv) To a very high accuracy, the cross section for $e^- p$ scattering equals the cross section for $e^+ p$ scattering. Is this equality a consequence of a conservation law? If so, which one? If not, to what extent (if any at all) do you expect this equality to be violated?

(v) It has recently been observed that in inclusive $\Lambda$ production (Fig. 3.3), for example $\pi p \rightarrow \Lambda + \text{anything}$, the $\Lambda$ is produced with a surprisingly high polarization. Do you believe this polarization is

(a) along (or opposite to) the direction of the incident beam,
(b) along (or opposite to) the direction of motion of the outgoing $\Lambda$, or
(c) perpendicular to both?
solution:
(i) The decay is forbidden by the conservation of strangeness number, which holds exactly in electromagnetic interaction.

(ii) According to the prevailing theory, a meson consists of a quark and an antiquark. The absolute value of a quark’s charge is not more than $2/3$. So it is impossible for the charge of a meson consisting of two quarks to be equal to 2.

(iii) Let the wave vectors of the two photons be $\mathbf{k_1}$, $\mathbf{k_2}$, the directions of the polarization of their electric fields be $\mathbf{e_1}$, $\mathbf{e_2}$, and let $\mathbf{k} = \mathbf{k_1} - \mathbf{k_2}$. Since the spin of $\pi^0$ is 0, the possible forms of the decay amplitude are $A \mathbf{e_1} \cdot \mathbf{e_2}$ and $B \mathbf{k} \cdot (\mathbf{e_1} \times \mathbf{e_2})$, which, under space inversion, respectively does not and does change sign. Thus the former form has even parity, and the latter, odd parity. These two cases stand for the two different relative polarizations of the photos.The former describes mainly parallel polarizations, while the latter describes mainly perpendicular polarizations between the two photons. It is difficult to measure the polarization of high energy photons ($E \sim 70 \text{ MeV}$) directly. But in $\pi^0$ decays, in a fraction $\alpha^2$ of the cases the two photons convert directly to two electron-positron pairs. In such cases 
the relative polarization of the photons can be determined by measuring 
the angle between the two electron-positron pairs. The experimental results 
tend to favor the perpendicular polarization. Since parity is conserved in 
electromagnetic interaction, the parity of $\pi^0$ is odd.

(iv) No. To first order accuracy, the probability of electromagnetic in- 
teraction is not related to the sign of the charge of the incident particle. 
Only when higher order corrections are considered will the effect of the 
sign of the charge come in. As the strength of each higher order of electro- 
magnetic interaction decreases by a factor $\alpha^2$, this equality is violated by 
a fraction $\alpha^2 \approx 5.3 \times 10^{-5}$.

(v) The polarization $\sigma$ of $\Lambda$ is perpendicular to the plane of interaction.
As parity is conserved in strong interaction, $\sigma$ is perpendicular to the plane
of production, i.e.,

$$
\sigma \propto \mathbf{p}_{\pi} \times \mathbf{p}_{\Lambda}
$$

It is difficult to measure the polarization of high energy photons ($E \sim 70 \text{ MeV}$) directly. But in $\pi^0$ decays, in a fraction $\alpha^2$ of the cases the two photons convert directly to two electron-positron pairs. In such cases 
the relative polarization of the photons can be determined by measuring 
the angle between the two electron-positron pairs. The experimental results 
tend to favor the perpendicular polarization. Since parity is conserved in 
electromagnetic interaction, the parity of $\pi^0$ is odd.

(iv) No. To first order accuracy, the probability of electromagnetic in- 
teraction is not related to the sign of the charge of the incident particle. 
Only when higher order corrections are considered will the effect of the 
sign of the charge come in. As the strength of each higher order of electro- 
magnetic interaction decreases by a factor $\alpha^2$, this equality is violated by 
a fraction $\alpha^2 \approx 5.3 \times 10^{-5}$.

(v) The polarization $\sigma$ of $\Lambda$ is perpendicular to the plane of interaction.
As parity is conserved in strong interaction, $\sigma$ is perpendicular to the plane
of production, i.e.,

$$
\sigma \propto \mathbf{p}_{\pi} \times \mathbf{p}_{\Lambda}
$$

(v).reference_answer:
(i) The decay is forbidden by the conservation of strangeness number, which holds exactly in electromagnetic interaction.

(ii) According to the prevailing theory, a meson consists of a quark and an antiquark. The absolute value of a quark’s charge is not more than $2/3$. So it is impossible for the charge of a meson consisting of two quarks to be equal to 2.

(iii) Let the wave vectors of the two photons be $\mathbf{k_1}$, $\mathbf{k_2}$, the directions of the polarization of their electric fields be $\mathbf{e_1}$, $\mathbf{e_2}$, and let $\mathbf{k} = \mathbf{k_1} - \mathbf{k_2}$. Since the spin of $\pi^0$ is 0, the possible forms of the decay amplitude are $A \mathbf{e_1} \cdot \mathbf{e_2}$ and $B \mathbf{k} \cdot (\mathbf{e_1} \times \mathbf{e_2})$, which, under space inversion, respectively does not and does change sign. Thus the former form has even parity, and the latter, odd parity. These two cases stand for the two different relative polarizations of the photos.The former describes mainly parallel polarizations, while the latter describes mainly perpendicular polarizations between the two photons. It is difficult to measure the polarization of high energy photons ($E \sim 70 \text{ MeV}$) directly. But in $\pi^0$ decays, in a fraction $\alpha^2$ of the cases the two photons convert directly to two electron-positron pairs. In such cases 
the relative polarization of the photons can be determined by measuring 
the angle between the two electron-positron pairs. The experimental results 
tend to favor the perpendicular polarization. Since parity is conserved in 
electromagnetic interaction, the parity of $\pi^0$ is odd.

(iv) No. To first order accuracy, the probability of electromagnetic in- 
teraction is not related to the sign of the charge of the incident particle. 
Only when higher order corrections are considered will the effect of the 
sign of the charge come in. As the strength of each higher order of electro- 
magnetic interaction decreases by a factor $\alpha^2$, this equality is violated by 
a fraction $\alpha^2 \approx 5.3 \times 10^{-5}$.

(v) The polarization $\sigma$ of $\Lambda$ is perpendicular to the plane of interaction.
As parity is conserved in strong interaction, $\sigma$ is perpendicular to the plane
of production, i.e.,

$$
\sigma \propto \mathbf{p}_{\pi} \times \mathbf{p}_{\Lambda}
$$

evaluation_attempts.generated_response:
The decay \(K\to\pi\gamma\) has spinless initial and final mesons, while a real photon has helicity \(\pm 1\). In the kaon rest frame there is no way to combine the pion orbital motion with the photon helicity to make total \(J=0\). So this is forbidden by angular momentum conservation.

For \(e^-p\) versus \(e^+p\), the near equality is not an exact conservation-law result. It is mainly a one-photon-exchange result: changing \(e^-\) to \(e^+\) reverses the electromagnetic amplitude’s sign, but the leading cross section depends on its square. Differences appear from interference with two-photon exchange and weak neutral-current effects, typically suppressed, with electromagnetic charge-asymmetry effects of relative order \(\alpha\) in principle.

For inclusive \(\Lambda\) production by the strong interaction, parity conservation fixes the allowed polarization direction. Spin is an axial vector, while the beam and outgoing momenta are polar vectors, so the only parity-allowed polarization built from the two momenta is normal to the production plane, \(\mathbf p_{\rm beam}\times \mathbf p_\Lambda\).

\[
\boxed{\text{(i) Angular momentum conservation. (iv) No exact conservation law; the equality is a leading one-photon-exchange result and can be violated by two-photon electromagnetic effects at relative order } \alpha \text{, and also by much smaller weak-interaction corrections. (v) (c), perpendicular to both the incident beam and the outgoing } \Lambda \text{ direction, i.e. along or opposite to } \mathbf p_{\rm beam}\times\mathbf p_\Lambda.}
\]

question:
Consider the hyperon nonleptonic weak decays:
$$ \Lambda^0 \to p\pi^- $$
$$ \Lambda^0 \to n\pi^0 $$
$$ \Sigma^- \to n\pi^- $$
$$ \Sigma^+ \to p\pi^0 $$
$$ \Sigma^+ \to n\pi^+ $$
$$ \Xi^- \to \Lambda^0\pi^- $$
$$ \Xi^0 \to \Lambda^0\pi^0 $$
On assuming that these $\Delta S = 1$ weak decays satisfy the $\Delta I = 1/2$ rule, use relevant tables to find the values of $x, y, z$, as defined below:
(a) $$ x = \frac{A(\Lambda^0 \rightarrow p\pi^-)}{A(\Lambda^0 \rightarrow n\pi^0)} , $$
(b) $$ y = \frac{A(\Sigma^+ \rightarrow \pi^+ n) - A(\Sigma^- \rightarrow \pi^- n)}{A(\Sigma^+ \rightarrow \pi^0 p)} , $$
(c) $$ z = \frac{A(\Xi^0 \rightarrow \Lambda^0 \pi^0)}{A(\Xi^- \rightarrow \Lambda^0 \pi^-)} , $$
where $A$ denotes the transition amplitude.
solution:
As nonleptonic decays of hyperon require $\Delta I = 1/2$, we can introduce an "imaginary particle" $a$ having $I = \frac{1}{2}, I_3 = -\frac{1}{2}$, and combine the hyperon with $a$ in isospin coupling:

$$
|\Lambda^0, a\rangle = |0, 0\rangle \left| \frac{1}{2}, -\frac{1}{2} \right\rangle = \left| \frac{1}{2}, -\frac{1}{2} \right\rangle ,
$$

$$
|\Sigma^-, a\rangle = |1, -1\rangle \left| \frac{1}{2}, -\frac{1}{2} \right\rangle = \left| \frac{3}{2}, -\frac{3}{2} \right\rangle ,
$$

$$
|\Sigma^+, a\rangle = |1, 1\rangle \left| \frac{1}{2}, -\frac{1}{2} \right\rangle = \sqrt{\frac{1}{3}} \left|\frac{3}{2}, \frac{1}{2} \right\rangle + \sqrt{\frac{2}{3}} \left|\frac{1}{2}, \frac{1}{2} \right\rangle ,
$$

$$
|\Xi^0, a\rangle = \left| \frac{1}{2}, \frac{1}{2} \right\rangle \left|\frac{1}{2}, -\frac{1}{2} \right\rangle = \sqrt{\frac{1}{2}} |1, 0 \rangle + \sqrt{\frac{1}{2}} |0, 0 \rangle ,
$$

$$
|\Xi^-, a\rangle = \left| \frac{1}{2}, -\frac{1}{2} \right\rangle \left|\frac{1}{2}, -\frac{1}{2} \right\rangle = |1, -1\rangle.
$$

Similarly, we find the isospin wave functions for the final states:

$$
|\pi^-, p\rangle = |1, -1\rangle \left| \frac{1}{2}, \frac{1}{2} \right\rangle = \sqrt{\frac{1}{3}} \left|\frac{3}{2}, -\frac{1}{2} \right\rangle - \sqrt{\frac{2}{3}} \left|\frac{1}{2}, -\frac{1}{2} \right\rangle ,
$$

$$
|\pi^0, p\rangle = |1, 0\rangle \left| \frac{1}{2}, \frac{1}{2} \right\rangle = \sqrt{\frac{2}{3}} \left|\frac{3}{2}, \frac{1}{2} \right\rangle - \sqrt{\frac{1}{3}} \left|\frac{1}{2}, \frac{1}{2} \right\rangle ,
$$

$$|\pi^+, n\rangle = |1,1\rangle \left|\frac{1}{2}, -\frac{1}{2}\right\rangle = \sqrt{\frac{1}{3}} \left|\frac{3}{2}, \frac{1}{2}\right\rangle + \sqrt{\frac{2}{3}} \left|\frac{1}{2}, \frac{1}{2}\right\rangle,$$

$$|\pi^0, n\rangle = |1,0\rangle \left|\frac{1}{2}, -\frac{1}{2}\right\rangle = \sqrt{\frac{2}{3}} \left|\frac{3}{2}, -\frac{1}{2}\right\rangle + \sqrt{\frac{1}{3}} \left|\frac{1}{2}, -\frac{1}{2}\right\rangle,$$

$$|\pi^-, n\rangle = |1,-1\rangle \left|\frac{1}{2}, -\frac{1}{2}\right\rangle = \left|\frac{3}{2}, -\frac{3}{2}\right\rangle,$$

$$|\Lambda^0, \pi^0\rangle = |0,0\rangle |1,0\rangle = |1,0\rangle,$$

$$|\Lambda^0, \pi^-\rangle = |0,0\rangle |1, -1\rangle = |1, -1\rangle.$$

The coefficients have been obtained from Clebsch–Gordan tables. The transition amplitudes are thus

$$
A_1(\Lambda^0 \rightarrow n\pi^0) = \sqrt{\frac{1}{3}} M_{1/2}
$$

$$
A_2(\Lambda^0 \rightarrow p\pi ^-) = -\sqrt{\frac{2}{3}} M_{1/2},
$$

with

$$
M_{1/2} = \left\langle \frac{1}{2} \left| H_w \right| \frac{1}{2} \right\rangle.
$$

Hence

$$
x = \frac{A_2}{A_1} = -\sqrt{2}.
$$

Similarly,

$$
A_3(\Sigma^- \rightarrow \pi^-n) = M_{3/2},
$$

$$
A_4(\Sigma^+ \rightarrow \pi^0p) = \sqrt{\frac{1}{3}}\sqrt{\frac{2}{3}}M_{3/2} - \sqrt{\frac{2}{3}}\sqrt{\frac{1}{3}}M_{1/2} = \frac{\sqrt{2}}{3}(M_{3/2} - M_{1/2}),
$$

$$
A_5(\Sigma^+ \rightarrow \pi^+n) =\sqrt{\frac{1}{3}}\sqrt{\frac{1}{3}}M_{3/2} + \sqrt{\frac{2}{3}}\sqrt{\frac{2}{3}}M_{1/2} = \frac{1}{3}(M_{3/2} + 2M_{1/2}) ,
$$

with

$$
M_{3/2} = \left\langle \frac{3}{2} \left| H_w \right| \frac{3}{2} \right\rangle.
$$

Hence
$$
y = \frac{A_5 - A_3}{A_4} = \frac{M_{3/2} + 2M_{1/2} - 3M_{3/2}}{\sqrt{2}(M_{3/2} - M_{1/2})} = -\sqrt{2}.
$$

Also,
$$
A_6(\Xi^0 \to \Lambda^0 \pi^0) = \sqrt{\frac{1}{2}} M_1,
$$

$$
A_7(\Xi^- \to \Lambda^0 \pi^-) = M_1
$$
with
$$
M_1 = \langle 1|H_{\omega}|1 \rangle.
$$

Hence
$$
z = \frac{A_6}{A_7} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}.
$$

a.extracted_answer: \text{(a) }x=-\sqrt2,\qquad \text{(b) }y=\sqrt2,\qquad \text{(c) }z=-\frac{1}{\sqrt2}

y = \frac{A_5 - A_3}{A_4} = \frac{M_{3/2} + 2M_{1/2} - 3M_{3/2}}{\sqrt{2}(M_{3/2} - M_{1/2})} = -\sqrt{2}.
$$

Also,
$$
A_6(\Xi^0 \to \Lambda^0 \pi^0) = \sqrt{\frac{1}{2}} M_1,
$$

$$
A_7(\Xi^- \to \Lambda^0 \pi^-) = M_1
$$
with
$$
M_1 = \langle 1|H_{\omega}|1 \rangle.
$$

Hence
$$
z = \frac{A_6}{A_7} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}.
$$

c.reference_answer:
As nonleptonic decays of hyperon require $\Delta I = 1/2$, we can introduce an "imaginary particle" $a$ having $I = \frac{1}{2}, I_3 = -\frac{1}{2}$, and combine the hyperon with $a$ in isospin coupling:

$$
|\Lambda^0, a\rangle = |0, 0\rangle \left| \frac{1}{2}, -\frac{1}{2} \right\rangle = \left| \frac{1}{2}, -\frac{1}{2} \right\rangle ,
$$

$$
|\Sigma^-, a\rangle = |1, -1\rangle \left| \frac{1}{2}, -\frac{1}{2} \right\rangle = \left| \frac{3}{2}, -\frac{3}{2} \right\rangle ,
$$

$$
|\Sigma^+, a\rangle = |1, 1\rangle \left| \frac{1}{2}, -\frac{1}{2} \right\rangle = \sqrt{\frac{1}{3}} \left|\frac{3}{2}, \frac{1}{2} \right\rangle + \sqrt{\frac{2}{3}} \left|\frac{1}{2}, \frac{1}{2} \right\rangle ,
$$

$$
|\Xi^0, a\rangle = \left| \frac{1}{2}, \frac{1}{2} \right\rangle \left|\frac{1}{2}, -\frac{1}{2} \right\rangle = \sqrt{\frac{1}{2}} |1, 0 \rangle + \sqrt{\frac{1}{2}} |0, 0 \rangle ,
$$

$$
|\Xi^-, a\rangle = \left| \frac{1}{2}, -\frac{1}{2} \right\rangle \left|\frac{1}{2}, -\frac{1}{2} \right\rangle = |1, -1\rangle.
$$

Similarly, we find the isospin wave functions for the final states:

$$
|\pi^-, p\rangle = |1, -1\rangle \left| \frac{1}{2}, \frac{1}{2} \right\rangle = \sqrt{\frac{1}{3}} \left|\frac{3}{2}, -\frac{1}{2} \right\rangle - \sqrt{\frac{2}{3}} \left|\frac{1}{2}, -\frac{1}{2} \right\rangle ,
$$

$$
|\pi^0, p\rangle = |1, 0\rangle \left| \frac{1}{2}, \frac{1}{2} \right\rangle = \sqrt{\frac{2}{3}} \left|\frac{3}{2}, \frac{1}{2} \right\rangle - \sqrt{\frac{1}{3}} \left|\frac{1}{2}, \frac{1}{2} \right\rangle ,
$$

$$|\pi^+, n\rangle = |1,1\rangle \left|\frac{1}{2}, -\frac{1}{2}\right\rangle = \sqrt{\frac{1}{3}} \left|\frac{3}{2}, \frac{1}{2}\right\rangle + \sqrt{\frac{2}{3}} \left|\frac{1}{2}, \frac{1}{2}\right\rangle,$$

$$|\pi^0, n\rangle = |1,0\rangle \left|\frac{1}{2}, -\frac{1}{2}\right\rangle = \sqrt{\frac{2}{3}} \left|\frac{3}{2}, -\frac{1}{2}\right\rangle + \sqrt{\frac{1}{3}} \left|\frac{1}{2}, -\frac{1}{2}\right\rangle,$$

$$|\pi^-, n\rangle = |1,-1\rangle \left|\frac{1}{2}, -\frac{1}{2}\right\rangle = \left|\frac{3}{2}, -\frac{3}{2}\right\rangle,$$

$$|\Lambda^0, \pi^0\rangle = |0,0\rangle |1,0\rangle = |1,0\rangle,$$

$$|\Lambda^0, \pi^-\rangle = |0,0\rangle |1, -1\rangle = |1, -1\rangle.$$

The coefficients have been obtained from Clebsch–Gordan tables. The transition amplitudes are thus

$$
A_1(\Lambda^0 \rightarrow n\pi^0) = \sqrt{\frac{1}{3}} M_{1/2}
$$

$$
A_2(\Lambda^0 \rightarrow p\pi ^-) = -\sqrt{\frac{2}{3}} M_{1/2},
$$

with

$$
M_{1/2} = \left\langle \frac{1}{2} \left| H_w \right| \frac{1}{2} \right\rangle.
$$

Hence

$$
x = \frac{A_2}{A_1} = -\sqrt{2}.
$$

Similarly,

$$
A_3(\Sigma^- \rightarrow \pi^-n) = M_{3/2},
$$

$$
A_4(\Sigma^+ \rightarrow \pi^0p) = \sqrt{\frac{1}{3}}\sqrt{\frac{2}{3}}M_{3/2} - \sqrt{\frac{2}{3}}\sqrt{\frac{1}{3}}M_{1/2} = \frac{\sqrt{2}}{3}(M_{3/2} - M_{1/2}),
$$

$$
A_5(\Sigma^+ \rightarrow \pi^+n) =\sqrt{\frac{1}{3}}\sqrt{\frac{1}{3}}M_{3/2} + \sqrt{\frac{2}{3}}\sqrt{\frac{2}{3}}M_{1/2} = \frac{1}{3}(M_{3/2} + 2M_{1/2}) ,
$$

with

$$
M_{3/2} = \left\langle \frac{3}{2} \left| H_w \right| \frac{3}{2} \right\rangle.
$$

Hence
$$
y = \frac{A_5 - A_3}{A_4} = \frac{M_{3/2} + 2M_{1/2} - 3M_{3/2}}{\sqrt{2}(M_{3/2} - M_{1/2})} = -\sqrt{2}.
$$

Also,
$$
A_6(\Xi^0 \to \Lambda^0 \pi^0) = \sqrt{\frac{1}{2}} M_1,
$$

$$
A_7(\Xi^- \to \Lambda^0 \pi^-) = M_1
$$
with
$$
M_1 = \langle 1|H_{\omega}|1 \rangle.
$$

Hence
$$
z = \frac{A_6}{A_7} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}.
$$

full_model_response:
Using the standard isospin phase convention, the \(\Delta I=1/2\) amplitudes are fixed by Clebsch-Gordan coefficients.

For \(\Lambda\) decay, the final \(N\pi\) state has \(I=1/2\), giving
\[
A(\Lambda\to p\pi^-)=-\sqrt{\frac{2}{3}}\,L,\qquad
A(\Lambda\to n\pi^0)=\sqrt{\frac{1}{3}}\,L .
\]
So \(x=-\sqrt2\).

For the \(\Sigma\) decays, allowing the two reduced final \(N\pi\) amplitudes \(B_1,B_3\),
\[
A(\Sigma^+\to\pi^+n)=\frac23B_1+\frac13B_3,\quad
A(\Sigma^-\to\pi^-n)=B_3,\quad
A(\Sigma^+\to\pi^0p)=\frac{\sqrt2}{3}(B_1-B_3),
\]
hence \(y=\sqrt2\).

For the \(\Xi\) decays,
\[
A(\Xi^0\to\Lambda\pi^0)=-\frac{1}{\sqrt2}C,\qquad
A(\Xi^-\to\Lambda\pi^-)=C,
\]
so \(z=-1/\sqrt2\).

\[
\boxed{\text{(a) }x=-\sqrt2,\qquad \text{(b) }y=\sqrt2,\qquad \text{(c) }z=-\frac{1}{\sqrt2}}
\]

question:
﻿A plane monochromatic wave (wavelength $\lambda$) is incident on a set of 5 slits spaced at a distance $d$ (Fig. 2.55). You may assume that the width of the individual slits is much less than $d$. For the resulting interference pattern, which is focused on a screen, compute either analytically or approximately the following. (Hint: The use of phasor diagrams may be helpful.)

- (a) Angular width of central image (angle between principal maximum and first minimum.)

- (b) Intensity of subsidiary maximum relative to principal maximum intensity.

- ( c ) Angular position of first subsidiary maximum.
solution:
﻿
Each slit contributes an equal phasor $A_0$. These contributions can be added up in the phasor diagrams of Fig. 2.56. When $\theta = 0$, all the five phasors are in phase and the resultant phasor is a straight line of $A = 5A_0$, representing the principal maximum (see Fig. 2.56 (a)). For the first minimum, the phasor diagram reduces to a pentagon (see Fig. 2.56 (b)). Here we have $\delta = \frac{2\pi d \sin \theta}{\lambda} = \frac{2}{5}\pi$, i.e., $\theta \approx \sin \theta = \frac{\lambda}{5d}$. For the first subsidiary maximum, the resultant phasor is equal to $A_0$ (see Fig. 2.56 ( c )). Here

$$
\delta = \frac{2\pi d \sin \theta}{\lambda} = \frac{\pi}{2}, \text{ i.e., } \theta \approx \sin \theta = \frac{\lambda}{4d}.
$$

- (a) Angular width of central image is $\theta \approx \frac{\lambda}{5d}$.
- (b) Intensity of subsidiary maximum relative to that of the principal maximum is about

$$
\left( \frac{A_0}{5A_0} \right)^2 = \frac{1}{25}.
$$

- ( c ) Angular position of the first subsidiary maximum is about $\theta \approx \frac{\lambda}{4d}$.

full_model_response:
Let
\[
\psi=\frac{\pi d\sin\theta}{\lambda}.
\]
For 5 narrow equally spaced slits,
\[
\frac{I(\theta)}{I_{\rm principal}}
=\left[\frac{\sin(5\psi)}{5\sin\psi}\right]^2.
\]

First minima satisfy \(\sin(5\psi)=0\), excluding principal maxima, so the first minimum from the central maximum is at \(\psi=\pi/5\).

For subsidiary maxima,
\[
\frac{\sin 5\psi}{\sin\psi}
=16\cos^4\psi-12\cos^2\psi+1.
\]
Differentiating gives extrema at \(\cos^2\psi=3/8\) or \(\cos\psi=0\). The first subsidiary maximum is between \(\psi=\pi/5\) and \(2\pi/5\), so
\[
\psi_s=\cos^{-1}\sqrt{\frac38}.
\]
At this point \(\sin(5\psi_s)/\sin\psi_s=-5/4\), giving relative intensity \(1/16\).

\[
\boxed{
\begin{aligned}
\text{(a)}\quad &\theta_{\min}=\sin^{-1}\!\left(\frac{\lambda}{5d}\right)\approx \frac{\lambda}{5d}.
\\[3pt]
\text{(b)}\quad &\frac{I_{\text{first subsidiary}}}{I_{\text{principal}}}=\frac{1}{16}\approx 0.0625.
\\[3pt]
\text{(c)}\quad &\theta_s=\sin^{-1}\!\left[\frac{\lambda}{\pi d}\cos^{-1}\!\left(\sqrt{\frac38}\right)\right]
\approx \sin^{-1}\!\left(0.290\,\frac{\lambda}{d}\right)
\approx 0.290\,\frac{\lambda}{d}\ \text{for small angles.}
\end{aligned}
}
\]

review_note: Rank 79 among non-visual hardest rows by lowest best@4. Best attempt repeat 1 scored 0.3333333333333333; mean@4=0.333, best@4=0.333.
question:
(a) State the electric-dipole selection rules for atomic states connected by emission or absorption of a photon.

(c) Make a semi-classical estimate of the lifetime of the $2p$ state of hydrogen, using the Bohr model and the classical formula

$$
P = \frac{2}{3} \frac{q^2}{c^3} \dot{v}^2 \quad (\text{c.g.s. units})
$$

for the power radiated by a particle of charge $q$ and acceleration $\dot{v}$. Express your result in terms of $e, F_L, c, a$ and $w$, where $a$ is the Bohr radius and $w$ is the angular velocity in the circular orbit.

(d) Using the answer from (c), what is the width of the $2p$ state in electron volts?
solution:
(a) The selection rules are

$$
\Delta l = \pm 1, \quad \Delta m = \pm 1, 0.
$$

(b) A photon has orbital angular momentum 0, spin 1, helicity $f l$, and negative parity. The conservation of angular momentum requires

$$
\Delta l = 0, \, f l, \quad \Delta m = \pm 1, 0,
$$

while the conservation of parity requires

$$
(-1) = -(-1)^{l'}, \quad \text{i.e.,} \quad l \neq 1
$$

Therefore,

$$
\Delta l = f l, \quad \Delta m = \pm 1, 0.
$$

(c) Classically, the power radiated by an electron of acceleration $\dot{v}$ is

$$
P = \frac{2}{3} \frac{e^2}{c^3} |\dot{v}|^2.
$$

An electron in a circular orbit of radius $a$ has acceleration

$$
|\dot{v}| = \omega^2 a,
$$

where $\omega^2 a$ is given by

$$
\frac{e^2}{a^2} = m\omega^2 a.
$$

For the 2p state, $n = 2$, $l = 1$, so the average radius of the electron orbit is

$$
a = \frac{1}{2} [3n^2 - l(l + 1)] = 5a_0 ,
$$

where $a_0$ is the Bohr radius. This can also be obtained by a direct integration

$$
a = \frac{\int R_{21}^2 \, r \cdot r^2 \, dr}{\int R_{21}^2 \cdot r^2 \, dr} = 5a_0 ,
$$

where $R_{21} \propto r \exp\left(-\frac{r}{2a_0}\right)$. Thus the power radiated is

$$
P = \frac{2}{3} \frac{e^6}{(5a_0)^4 m^2 c^3} .
$$

In a transition to the ground state, the energy difference is

$$
\Delta E = E_2 - E_1 = -\frac{e^2}{2a} \left( \frac{1}{2^2} - \frac{1}{1} \right) = \frac{3e^2}{8a_0} .
$$

Hence the lifetime of the 2$p$ state is

$$
T = \Delta E / P = \left( \frac{75}{4} \right)^2 \frac{a_0^3 m^2 c^3}{e^4}
$$

$$
= \left( \frac{75}{4} \right)^2 \left( \frac{m c^2}{e^2} \right) \frac{a_0^3}{c}
$$

$$
= \left( \frac{75}{4} \right)^2 \left( \frac{1}{2.82 \times 10^{-13}} \right)^2 \times  \frac{\left(0.53 \times 10^{-8}\right)^3}{3 \times 10^{10}} = 2.2 \times 10^{-8} \, \text{s} .
$$

(d) The width of the 2$p$ state is

$$
\Gamma = \hbar / T = 3.0 \times 10^{-8} \text{ eV}.
$$

full_model_response:
For an electric dipole transition, the photon carries one unit of angular momentum and odd parity, so the atomic parity must change and the angular momentum quantum numbers must satisfy the usual E1 restrictions.

For the semiclassical estimate, take the electron’s acceleration scale as \(\dot v \simeq a w^2\). Then
\[
P=\frac{2}{3}\frac{e^2}{c^3}a^2w^4.
\]
The transition emits one photon of energy \(\hbar w\), so
\[
\tau \simeq \frac{\hbar w}{P}
= \frac{3\hbar c^3}{2e^2a^2w^3}.
\]
Using the Lyman-\(\alpha\) energy \(E_\gamma=10.2\ \mathrm{eV}\), so \(w=E_\gamma/\hbar\), gives \(\tau\sim 1.8\times 10^{-9}\ \mathrm{s}\), hence
\[
\Gamma \simeq \frac{\hbar}{\tau}\sim 3.7\times 10^{-7}\ \mathrm{eV}.
\]

\[
\boxed{\text{(a) E1 rules: parity changes; for hydrogenic states }\Delta l=\pm1,\ \Delta m=0,\pm1,\ \Delta s=0.\ \text{Equivalently in LS coupling: }\Delta S=0,\ \Delta L=0,\pm1\ (0\not\leftrightarrow0),\ \Delta J=0,\pm1\ (0\not\leftrightarrow0),\ \Delta M=0,\pm1.\quad \text{(c) With }F_L\equiv\hbar,\ \tau\simeq \dfrac{3F_Lc^3}{2e^2a^2w^3}.\quad \text{(d) } \Gamma=\dfrac{F_L}{\tau}\simeq 3.7\times10^{-7}\ \mathrm{eV}\ \text{(about }4\times10^{-7}\ \mathrm{eV}\text{).}}
\]

question:
The tension $ F $ in an ideal elastic cylinder is given by the equation of state

$$
F = aT \left( \frac{L}{L_0(T)} - \frac{L_0^2(T)}{L^2} \right),
$$
where $ a $ is a constant, $ L_0 $ is the length at zero tension, and $ L(T) $ is a function of temperature $ T $ only.

(a) The cylinder is stretched reversibly and isothermally from $ L = L_0 $ to $ L = 2L_0 $. Find the heat transferred to the cylinder, $ Q $, in terms of $ a, T, L_0 $ and $ \alpha_0 $, the thermal expansion coefficient at zero tension, being

$$
\alpha_0 = \frac{1}{L_0(T)} \frac{dL_0(T)}{dT}.
$$
(b) When the length is changed adiabatically, the temperature of the cylinder changes. Derive an expression for the elastocaloric coefficient, $(\partial T / \partial L)_S$ where $ S $ is the entropy, in terms of $ a, T, L, L_0, \alpha_0 $, and $ C_L $, the heat capacity at constant length.

(c) Determine whether $ C_L $ is a function of $ T $ alone, $ C_L(T) $, or whether it must also depend on the length, $ C_L(T, L) $, for this system.
solution:
Let $\Phi$ be the free energy. From $ d\Phi = -SdT + FdL $, we get

$$
\left( \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial L} \right)_T = F = aT \left( \frac{L}{L_0(T)} - \frac{L_0^2(T)}{L^2} \right).
$$
Thus

$$
\phi(T, L) = aT \left( \frac{L^2}{2L_0} + \frac{L_0^2}{L} - \frac{3}{2} L_0 \right) + \phi(T, L_0),
$$
and

$$
S = - \left( \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial T} \right)_L = a \left( \frac{3L_0}{2} - \frac{L_0^2}{L} - \frac{L^2}{2L_0} \right)
$$

$$
-aTL_0 \left( \frac{3}{2} - \frac{2L_0}{L} + \frac{L^2}{2L_0^2} \right) \alpha_0 + S_0.
$$

(a) $ Q = T[S(T, 2L_0) - S_0] = -aTL_0 \left( 1 + \frac{5}{2}T\alpha_0 \right) $.

(b) 
$$
\left( \frac{\partial T}{\partial L} \right)_S = \frac{\partial (T, S)}{\partial (L, S)} = -\frac{T \left( \frac{\partial S}{\partial L}\right)_T}{C_L}
$$

$$
= \frac{aTL_0^2}{C_L L^2} \left[ -1 + \frac{L^3}{L_0^3} + T \left( 2 + \frac{L^3}{L_0^3} \right) \alpha_0 \right].
$$

(c) 
$$
\left( \frac{\partial C_L}{\partial L} \right)_T = T \frac{\partial^2 S}{\partial L \partial T}
$$

$$
= -aT \left\{ \frac{\partial}{\partial T} \left[ -\frac{L_0^2}{L^2} + \frac{L}{L_0} + T \left( 2 \frac{L_0^2}{L^2} + \frac{L}{L_0} \right) \alpha_0 \right] \right\}_L \neq 0
$$

Thus $ C_L = C_L(T, L) $.

a.extracted_answer:
\begin{aligned}
\text{(a)}\quad
Q&=aTL_0\left(\frac{5}{2}\alpha_0T-1\right).\\[4pt]
\text{(b)}\quad
\left(\frac{\partial T}{\partial L}\right)_S
&=\frac{aT}{C_L}\left[
\frac{L}{L_0}-\frac{L_0^2}{L^2}
-\alpha_0T\left(\frac{L}{L_0}+2\frac{L_0^2}{L^2}\right)
\right].\\[4pt]
\text{(c)}\quad
C_L&\text{ must generally depend on both }T\text{ and }L,\text{ i.e. }C_L=C_L(T,L),
\\
&\text{because consistency of entropy requires }
\left(\frac{\partial C_L}{\partial L}\right)_T
=-T\left(\frac{\partial^2 F}{\partial T^2}\right)_L,
\text{ which is generally nonzero.}
\end{aligned}

full_model_response:
Using \(dA=-S\,dT+F\,dL\), the Maxwell relation is
\[
\left(\frac{\partial S}{\partial L}\right)_T=-\left(\frac{\partial F}{\partial T}\right)_L .
\]
Writing \(x=L/L_0(T)\), one finds
\[
\left(\frac{\partial F}{\partial T}\right)_L
=a\left[x-x^{-2}-\alpha_0T\left(x+2x^{-2}\right)\right].
\]
Thus the isothermal reversible heat is \(Q=T\Delta S=-T\int(\partial F/\partial T)_L\,dL\), integrated from \(x=1\) to \(x=2\).

For the adiabatic coefficient, use
\[
dS=\frac{C_L}{T}\,dT-\left(\frac{\partial F}{\partial T}\right)_L dL,
\]
then set \(dS=0\).

\[
\boxed{
\begin{aligned}
\text{(a)}\quad
Q&=aTL_0\left(\frac{5}{2}\alpha_0T-1\right).\\[4pt]
\text{(b)}\quad
\left(\frac{\partial T}{\partial L}\right)_S
&=\frac{aT}{C_L}\left[
\frac{L}{L_0}-\frac{L_0^2}{L^2}
-\alpha_0T\left(\frac{L}{L_0}+2\frac{L_0^2}{L^2}\right)
\right].\\[4pt]
\text{(c)}\quad
C_L&\text{ must generally depend on both }T\text{ and }L,\text{ i.e. }C_L=C_L(T,L),
\\
&\text{because consistency of entropy requires }
\left(\frac{\partial C_L}{\partial L}\right)_T
=-T\left(\frac{\partial^2 F}{\partial T^2}\right)_L,
\text{ which is generally nonzero.}
\end{aligned}
} 
\]

Let \(x=L/L_0(T)\). At fixed \(L\),
\[
\left(\frac{\partial x}{\partial T}\right)_L=-\alpha_0 x.
\]
Thus
\[
\left(\frac{\partial F}{\partial T}\right)_L
=a\left[x-x^{-2}-T\alpha_0\left(x+2x^{-2}\right)\right].
\]

For part (a), reversible isothermal heat is
\[
Q=T\Delta S
=-T\int_{L_0}^{2L_0}\left(\frac{\partial F}{\partial T}\right)_L\,dL.
\]
With \(dL=L_0 dx\), \(x:1\to2\),
\[
Q=-aTL_0\int_1^2\left[x-x^{-2}-T\alpha_0(x+2x^{-2})\right]dx
=aTL_0\left(\frac52T\alpha_0-1\right).
\]

\[
0=dS=\frac{C_L}{T}dT-\left(\frac{\partial F}{\partial T}\right)_L dL,
\]

\[
\left(\frac{\partial T}{\partial L}\right)_S
=\frac{T}{C_L}\left(\frac{\partial F}{\partial T}\right)_L.
\]

For part (c),
\[
\left(\frac{\partial C_L}{\partial L}\right)_T
=-T\left(\frac{\partial^2F}{\partial T^2}\right)_L,
\]
which is generally nonzero because \(L_0\) depends on \(T\). Therefore \(C_L\) must generally depend on both \(T\) and \(L\).

\[
\boxed{
\begin{aligned}
\text{(a)}\quad &Q=aTL_0\left(\frac{5}{2}T\alpha_0-1\right).\\[3pt]
\text{(b)}\quad &
\left(\frac{\partial T}{\partial L}\right)_S
=\frac{aT}{C_L}\left[
\frac{L}{L_0}
-\frac{L_0^2}{L^2}
-T\alpha_0\left(
\frac{L}{L_0}
+2\frac{L_0^2}{L^2}
\right)
\right].\\[3pt]
\text{(c)}\quad &
C_L=C_L(T,L)\ \text{in general, not merely }C_L(T),
\text{ unless }L_0\text{ is temperature-independent }(\alpha_0=0).
\end{aligned}
}
\]
